position_summary: Claude correctly stopped treating the sandbox access-denied results as machine facts and accepted most of the substantive process risks. However, the revised narrative and the artifact diverge badly: several accepted fixes are stated in the disposition but not actually reflected in the runbook, and the critical gates are still not ordered precisely enough to be executable under time pressure.

findings.title: Gate ordering is internally inconsistent because GATE-GPU depends on a successful install
findings.evidence: Claude says GATE-0 and GATE-GPU occur "before any host mutation" / "before any host changes," but GATE-GPU requires running a CUDA operation "from inside a Claude Science task," which cannot happen until Claude Science is installed, authenticated, and able to launch tasks.
findings.concern_path: A solo builder may interpret both gates as pre-install blockers, then get stuck because the GPU gate is impossible to run before install. Or they may skip the gate entirely after install because its actual place in the sequence was never made explicit.
findings.suggested_action: Split gates into phases: GATE-0 before any install or data movement; GATE-1 install-source/managed-endpoint safe execution; GATE-RUNTIME after install/sign-in; GATE-GPU after Claude Science task execution works, but before any local-GPU-dependent workflow or model placement decision.

findings.title: The artifact still contains the rejected operational steps, so the accepted fixes are not executable
findings.evidence: Section 5 still says `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install-claude-science.sh | bash` and `claude-science serve ... --detached`; section 6 still treats distro-level `nvidia-smi` as the GPU validation; section 3 still says model weights go to D: and bubblewrap "will not run" on `/mnt/d`; section 8 rollback remains the old minimal rollback.
findings.concern_path: Under time pressure, the operator will follow the artifact, not the debate disposition. That means the blind installer, weak persistence, wrong GPU acceptance test, coarse D: placement rule, and incomplete rollback all remain live failure modes.
findings.suggested_action: Regenerate the runbook so the accepted changes are present in the actual numbered steps, risks, validation checklist, and rollback section. Treat the current artifact as stale until that rewrite is done.

findings.title: Managed-endpoint interference remains an open question instead of a concrete preflight
findings.evidence: Claude asks in open question 4 whether VCU CrowdStrike/KACE/Defender interference should be pre-checked, but the revised plan does not define what to check, what evidence is sufficient, or what to do if the installer, sandboxing, localhost service, or token-bearing files are quarantined.
findings.concern_path: The install can partially succeed, fail silently, or be quarantined after profile/service changes. The team may burn time debugging Claude Science when the real blocker is endpoint policy or AV behavior.
findings.suggested_action: Add a managed-endpoint preflight before running the installer: download-only test, AV/quarantine check, WSL process/sandbox allow check, localhost port allow check, and a defined escalation/fallback path if any step is blocked.

findings.title: Persistence design names mechanisms but omits the service contract needed to operate it
findings.evidence: Claude proposes a systemd user service with `WantedBy=default.target` plus `loginctl enable-linger`, or a Windows Task Scheduler fallback, but gives no unit file, restart policy, log location, readiness check, port-conflict behavior, token lifecycle handling, or WSL shutdown/reboot validation.
findings.concern_path: A nominal service may start once but fail across reboot, sleep/resume, expired auth, or port conflicts. Without logs and restart semantics, failures become opaque during the hackathon.
findings.suggested_action: Specify the actual service unit or scheduled task, including `ExecStart`, `Restart=on-failure`, log capture, health check command, restart command, port conflict handling, and an acceptance test after Windows reboot and WSL shutdown.

findings.title: Audit capture of the installer is underspecified and may contaminate the repo
findings.evidence: Claude says to "Capture the script + resolved version to the repo for audit," but AGENTS.md forbids committing raw data or secrets and emphasizes fresh hackathon work. The installer may contain third-party code, URLs, tokens, account-specific state, or license-sensitive content once resolved.
findings.concern_path: The team may accidentally check in an installer artifact, generated logs, or auth-adjacent material. Even if no secret is present, vendoring an opaque installer into the project muddles provenance and review scope.
findings.suggested_action: Store installer audit material in an ignored `memory/install-audit/` or similar local path, record only hashes, source URL, timestamp, version, and a short inspection summary in tracked docs unless the license and contents are explicitly safe to commit.

escalated_findings.rationale: Accepted in prose, but the artifact still contains the exact unverified installer URL and command. GATE-0 is not written as the first executable step with concrete pass/fail evidence for docs, CLI name, flags, and account entitlement.
Accepted in prose, but section 5 still uses blind `curl | bash`; fetch-inspect-run is only mentioned in a mitigation note. The operational runbook still points the user at the unsafe path.
Accepted in prose, but post-install validation still lists distro-level `nvidia-smi` rather than a CUDA operation inside a Claude Science task. The actual acceptance criterion remains too weak.

Accepted in prose, but the artifact still launches with `--detached` and leaves systemd as an open question. There is no concrete service unit, logging, restart policy, or reboot/sleep acceptance test.
Accepted in prose, but section 3 still says large model weights go to D: and local GPU workflows should store weights on D:. The corrected access-pattern rule was not propagated into the artifact.

Accepted in prose, but section 3 still asserts bubblewrap will not run on `/mnt/d`, and section 6 only checks simple file read from D:. The promised read/write/symlink/mmap/sandbox path tests are not in the artifact.

Accepted in prose, but rollback section still only restores `.wslconfig`, deletes `~/.local`/profile entry, and deletes D: data. The pre/post inventory and service/cache/token/log/package cleanup are not specified in the actual rollback procedure.
Accepted in prose, but the artifact still contains non-ASCII punctuation such as `·`, arrows, and typographic quotes. The ASCII normalization requirement has not been applied to the runbook.

dropped_findings.rationale: Per the supplied live user-shell transcript, WSL2 and Ubuntu access work for the real user. The previous access-denied result was a reviewer sandbox boundary, not a machine blocker.

Claude gave a concrete project-level resolution: Researcher-primary, with the Builder app as the vehicle. The remaining need is documentation cleanup, not an unresolved track decision blocking the install plan.
The live user-shell transcript verifies `free -h` shows 31 GiB and `nproc` shows 16 after WSL restart. The runtime memory concern is resolved.
The live user-shell transcript verifies disk media types: C: on NVMe SSD and D: on SATA HDD. The reviewer sandbox denial should not be treated as contrary evidence.

recommended_next_question: Can Claude produce a single updated runbook whose numbered steps enforce this exact sequence: GATE-0 docs/entitlement, managed-endpoint preflight, fetch-inspect-run install, service-based persistence with reboot validation, Claude Science task smoke test, then GATE-GPU CUDA-in-task before any local-GPU workflow?
